schools.summary: Genesis/Bĕrēshîth echo; the timeless 'was' (ēn) reaching into eternity; anarthrous theos = qualitative full deity, not 'a god'.
The essential (not written) Word; his eternal divinity grounds the necessity of redemption through the Son.
Augustinian: the natural man cannot perceive it; the verse is grasped by faith ('letters of gold').
Chrysostom: it is God who speaks through John, so we need not trace the evangelist's background.
The Word existed before all things began to be made.

debates.question: The sense of 'beginning' — the start of creation, or beyond time into eternity?

sides.position: the Genesis beginning of creation
reaching past creation into eternity

outliers.note: Alexandrian: catalogues the many scriptural senses of 'beginning' and reads Christ himself as the Beginning/Wisdom.

themes: the Word with God / distinct person

schools.summary: A guarded restatement of v.1 to prevent mistake; the Word a distinct person, eternally with God before creation.
Condenses the three assertions; the Word 'was before all time,' eternally with the Father.
Hilary: refers all to the one unbegotten God, guarding the oneness of God without obliterating the Person.
Repeats and contracts the three points of v.1 into one.
egeneto (came-to-be) of all things vs the timeless ēn of the Word; 'all things'=the universe, no exception (Col 1:16; Heb 1:2; Rev 4:11).
Deity proved from the work of creation; the name of God profits nothing unless faith feels it by experience.
Creation requires omnipotence, so a 'delegated' creatorship is absurd — Christ and the Father are one.
Chrysostom: 'nothing made without him' includes the intelligible, not only Moses' visible creation.
Origen weighs the senses of 'beginning'; Cyril of Jerusalem rebukes curiosity about thrones/dominions, themselves Christ's workmanship.
'Life' in the widest sense: the Logos creates and sustains all life (Gen 2:7; Heb 1:3; John 11:25).
From creation to preservation — life originally and independently in him; the regular order of nature.
Theophylact with Augustine: 'light of men' is the intellectual light of the rational soul, given to man as God's image.
The light is wisdom/holiness/happiness in man's original state; the animal life-principle is not itself the saving light.

debates.question: How wide is 'life' — the sustaining of all creation, or specifically the spiritual life/light of men?

sides.position: life in the widest sense, the creation and preservation of all things
the intellectual/spiritual light proper to rational souls

outliers.note: Reads v.4 as the prologue's pivot from creation to redemption — the Word restores life to man dead in trespasses.

themes: the Word as source of life

schools.summary: Augustine: the darkness fails to perceive the light — the blind man present to the sun yet absent from it.
The light 'keeps shining' (linear present); katelaben read as 'overcame/suppressed' (Clarke, Lightfoot) — the light breaks out through the shadows.
Men blind by nature; the light shines into a self-made darkness of vanity and lies.
Origen distinguishes the light shining in darkness (not overtaken) from the light in which there is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5).

debates.question: The sense of katelaben — did the darkness fail to 'comprehend' (perceive) the light, or fail to 'overcome' (suppress) it?

sides.position: failed to perceive/receive it
failed to overcome/extinguish/hinder it

outliers.note: Distinguishes two lights — the one that shines in darkness unovertaken vs. the one in which there is no darkness at all.

schools.summary: John 'came into being' (egeneto) vs the Word who 'was'; a man emphatically opposed to the Word, yet truly sent from God.
Augustine: a great man sent to witness that the One whose Godhead was concealed in flesh is more than man.
Chrysostom: as a 'messenger,' John's words are not his own but of Him who sent him.
God sent the herald before his Christ that men might more willingly receive salvation; John's extraordinary birth and ministry.
Henry: the name John signifies 'gracious.'
Origen asks the 'whence and whither' of John's sending — to Israel, and in the deeper sense into the world.
'Witness' (marturia) is a key Johannine word; its aim is conviction unto belief (cf. John 20:31).
John's calling was to prepare a Church for Christ, inviting all and not coming on his own account.
Origen: against those who deny Christ was foretold — the Son does receive witnesses.
John's testimony interwoven with the evangelist's, pointing men to the Lamb.
John was 'a light' (a lamp, John 5:35; cf. Matt 5:14) but not 'the Light' (to phōs); his office emphatically restated.
Calvin: the warning lest John's brightness obscure Christ — men gazing on the dawn neglect the sun.
'True'=genuine/archetypal, not true-vs-false; debate over whether 'coming into the world' modifies 'every man' or 'the Light.'
Calvin: Christ shines from and by himself; all else borrows its splendour from him.
Augustine: man enlightened is called light, but the true Light is that which enlightens.
A universal illumination — natural conscience (Wesley) and reason as a ray of the Logos (Clarke).

debates.question: Does 'that cometh into the world' qualify 'every man' or 'the true Light'?

sides.position: every man (Hebrew idiom for all who are born)
the true Light was coming into the world

debates.question: In what sense does the Light enlighten 'every man'?

sides.position: a universal natural light of conscience/reason
the saving light now manifested

schools.summary: The imperfect 'was' = continuous pre-incarnate existence in the universe (Robertson); Barnes alone refers it to the Incarnation.
An indictment of ingratitude: men drink from the stream and never think of the fountain.
Clarke: Christ in the world from creation, governing it, speaking by prophets, appearing as the angel of Jehovah.
Augustine: 'world' names both the creation and 'the lovers of the world' who knew him not.
Theophylact wields the verse against Manichaean (evil world) and Arian (Son a creature) errors.
Cyril of Jerusalem: Christ the Maker of things visible and invisible (Col 1:16).

debates.question: Does 'he was in the world' mean pre-incarnate/providential presence or the Incarnation?

sides.position: continuous pre-incarnate presence throughout history
the Incarnation, dwelling among men

schools.summary: Neuter ta idia ('his own home,' cf. John 19:27) vs masculine hoi idioi ('his own people/intimates') who received him not.
Rejection set against election: Israel his heritage by creation and choice, yet refusing him — the desperate malice of men.
Christ came to his own country, city, temple; his own people received him not.
Chrysostom: he who fills all things 'came' purely in condescension, not for his own good.
Cyril of Jerusalem: fulfils Mal 3:1 — 'the Lord shall come to His own temple.'

debates.question: What is the scope of 'his own' — the whole world he made, or Judea/Israel his chosen heritage?

sides.position: the world, his own by creation
Judea/Israel, his peculiar heritage

schools.summary: exousia is 'right/privilege' rather than 'power'; the dignity of sonship now open to all nations.
The universal 'as many as' implies contrast with Jewish boasting in descent — faith, not blood, receives.
Jews or Gentiles, the moment they believe they are sons; the Spirit of adoption cries 'Abba' (Rom 8:15).
Cyril of Jerusalem: believers are sons by adoption of grace, the Son alone Son by nature.
Regeneration, the new birth of John 3:3; 'bloods' linked to Jewish circumcision/passover notions; descent from pious parents avails nothing.
Gill: regeneration, a partaking of the divine nature, the evidence of sonship.
The threefold negation — not ancestry, nor natural generation, nor human adoption, but of God by his Spirit.
Cyril of Jerusalem: adoption of grace, beside the Son's sonship by nature.

outliers.note: Records the ancient singular variant ('who was born,' Old Latin/Irenaeus/Tertullian) referring the clause to Christ and the Virgin Birth, while holding the plural (of believers) as the true text.

schools.summary: egeneto ('became', the historic Incarnation) over against ēn; anarthrous 'flesh' = full human nature, the whole man; explicitly anti-Docetic (Ellicott).
The Son alone — not Father or Spirit — clothed with our flesh and shown openly; continuity with the Word of v.1.
Chrysostom: we are made sons because the Word became flesh; his glory was seen only through the Incarnation.
'Flesh' = the whole man; the beholding 'we' are the apostles; grace and truth come to us who are by nature otherwise.

debates.question: The sense of 'flesh' (sarx) here.

sides.position: the whole man / full human nature
canvassed as possibly 'corrupt nature' or 'the body' before settling on the whole man

schools.summary: 'after me' in time, 'preferred before me' in rank, 'before me' in pre-existence; historic present marturei, abiding perfect kekragen.
The present tense marks a continuing testimony, ever resounding in men's ears.
Alcuin: the witness declares both Christ's human excellence and the eternity of his Godhead.
Chrysostom: John witnessed without prior acquaintance, so not out of gratitude.
Wesley: 'preferred before me' in office, 'before me' in nature; John knew him at the baptism.
Henry: as prophets cried aloud to show sin, John cried aloud to show the Saviour.
The evangelist's words (not the Baptist's), tied to v.14; plērōma the Pauline term for the fulness of God in Christ (Col 1:19; 2:9; Eph 1:23).
Calvin: Christ's office contains all blessings — seek no part of salvation elsewhere; the fountain flows when faith opens the channel.
Origen: a continuation of the Baptist's testimony, the ground of 'he was before me.'
'Grace for grace' = blessing heaped upon blessing, immeasurable grace.

debates.question: Are these the Baptist's words or the evangelist's?

sides.position: the Baptist's, continuing his witness
the evangelist's, connected to v.14

debates.question: The sense of 'grace for grace'

sides.position: grace upon grace, accumulation of blessing
grace for the propagation of grace (apostleship)

schools.summary: edothē/dia Mōuseōs vs egeneto/dia Iēsou; first use of the full name 'Jesus Christ,' identifying the Logos with Jesus the Messiah; the law preparatory (Rom 4:15; Acts 15:10).
An anticipation meeting Jewish over-esteem of Moses; the law convicts and condemns but gives no strength or forgiveness — Christ's power exalted.
Wesley: grace opposed to wrath, truth to the law's shadows; Clarke: Christ the mediator of the new covenant (Heb 8:6; Gal 3:19).
God invisible to the physical eye (Exod 33:20; Deut 4:12; Col 1:15); the variant 'only begotten God' vs 'only begotten Son.'
Chrysostom: dwelling 'in the bosom' is far more than seeing — the Son knows the Father completely, not partially.
Calvin: the knowledge of God is the door to all blessings, and it comes only through Christ — seek all things from him.
'Seen' = 'known' (1 John 3:6); none fully knows God but through Christ, though believers see him by faith.
Cyril of Jerusalem: OT theophanies saw 'the likeness of the glory,' not the incorporeal God himself.

debates.question: The sense of 'hath seen' God

sides.position: bodily/physical sight (none has ever had it)
full knowledge ('seen'='known')

themes: in the bosom of the Father

schools.summary: 'The Jews' = the nation's representative hierarchy in opposition; this testimony follows the Prologue and assumes the Synoptic baptism.
Origen: priests and Levites sent as kin to the priestly Baptist; the second of his testimonies.
Calvin disputes 'hatred' as the motive — it was the natural duty of examining a new teacher.
Augustine: John the friend of the Bridegroom, zealous not for himself, granted to point Christ out.
Henry: the Sanhedrin, guides of the people, must themselves send to inquire.

debates.question: What was the motive of the deputation?

sides.position: the official duty of examining a new teacher
hostility or a flattering readiness to honour him

schools.summary: The emphatic triple 'confessed/denied not/confessed' and the emphatic pronoun: 'It is not I who am the Christ.'
An open confession without ambiguity or hypocrisy, expressly denying the Messianic claim.
Clarke: John renounces himself that Jesus may be all in all; he had nothing but what he received.
John denies being the personal/returned Elijah, while being the Elijah of prophecy 'in spirit and power' (Luke 1:17); 'that prophet' = Deut 18:15.
Calvin: the Jews' error of transmigration — they expected the very Elijah of Ahab's day.
Origen frames the crux via Jesus' 'if ye will receive it, this is Elijah.'
Wesley: not the Tishbite in person; the scribes taught Elijah must come (Matt 17:10).

debates.question: How can John deny being Elijah when Jesus affirms it?

sides.position: he is not the personal/returned Elijah the Tishbite
he is the Elijah of prophecy in spirit and power

schools.summary: Origen: 'we know what you are not; now tell us of yourself.'
The shortened, urgent question; they must not return to their senders empty-handed.
Gill: after three negatives, they press for a positive self-account.
John applies Isa 40:3 to himself; he is 'a voice, not to be inquired about but heard,' his personality vanishing before the Coming One.
The prophecy proves his commission — he does nothing but what God commanded, under the Spirit's direction.
Wesley: 'nothing but a voice' that, once it has uttered its thought, dies into air.
Cyril of Jerusalem joins Isa 40:3 with its sequel — 'your redemption hath drawn nigh.'
In John, with the Sadducees never named, 'Pharisees' approaches the sense of 'Sanhedrin'; the detail marks the writers' honesty.
Origen: the Pharisees come with a harder temper than the mild priests and Levites.
Calvin: men of authority, who therefore question his office and charge him with innovation.
Wesley: the Pharisees tenacious of old customs and jealous of any innovation.
Calvin: a syllogism of three offices — only Messiah, Elijah, or the prophet could institute such a rite, so John's baptizing seems unlawful novelty.
Proselyte baptism existed for Gentiles; the scandal is that John baptizes Jews/Pharisees as if impure Gentiles (Ezek 36:25).
The procedural objection: John baptizes without Sanhedrin commission, and baptizes Jews, not only heathen.
Origen weighs whether this is a distinct, third testimony.
John deflects rather than defends — mere water-baptism, preparatory; 'whom ye know not' is the tragedy of v.11, though John had already baptized Jesus.
Calvin: by sending them to the present Christ, John proves he is the true voice announcing the Church's renewal.
Origen defends the reply's relevance against Heracleon.
Clarke: the One whose forerunner John is now dwells in Judea and will shortly appear.
The clause 'preferred before me' is a scribal insertion from vv.15,30; loosing the sandal-thong was a slave's lowest office.
John follows Christ in time but stands far below him in rank; the Baptist is only a herald sent on Christ's account.
Cyril of Jerusalem turns the 'not worthy' aside to Paul's like confession of unworthiness.

outliers.note: Reads the verse homiletically through Paul's unworthiness rather than commenting on John the Baptist.

schools.summary: 'Bethany beyond Jordan' is the true text on all Greek MSS; 'Bethabara' is Origen's later geographic conjecture.
Origen, finding Bethany too far from the river, argues for Bethabara, 'house of preparation.'
Chrysostom holds 'the more correct copies have Bethabara' and notes John's bold public testimony.
Calvin leaves the spelling open; the place authenticates the narrative before a great crowd.

debates.question: Did John write 'Bethany' or 'Bethabara' beyond Jordan?

sides.position: Bethany (original reading of the manuscripts)
Bethabara (geographic/etymological reading)

schools.summary: The Lamb fulfils the daily sacrifice, Passover, and Isaiah 53; the present tense 'taketh away' is a continual bearing of the world's sin.
Christ's chief office: by his death he propitiates the Father; 'of the world' is offered to all but received by faith, overturning human satisfactions.
Augustine: only Christ is the spotless Lamb, not conceived in sin; no sinful minister can take away sin, Christ alone.
Sin and the world are of equal extent; the atonement reaches all mankind, not Israel only.
Origen allegorises the lamb as the soul's daily reasonable sacrifice, morning and evening.
Matthew Henry's pastoral turn: if he takes away the sin of the world, why not my sin?

outliers.note: Alexandrian allegory of the daily lamb as the soul's reasonable sacrifice, beyond the plain sacrificial type.

themes: sin of the world / scope of redemption

schools.summary: The third repetition (vv.15,27) of a proverbial saying; 'on behalf of whom' points to Jesus now present.
Calvin's three claims: John prior only in time, Christ preferred in glory, grounded in his eternal pre-existent majesty.
'I knew him not' disclaims personal intimacy (they lived apart), ruling out collusion; the aim of baptism was to manifest Messiah to Israel.
John testifies by the Spirit and God's command, not human favour; being 'sent' shows sacraments are instituted by God alone.
Providence kept the kinsmen apart so John's witness could not be suspected of partiality.
Augustine: a real dove, not Christ's first reception of the Spirit but its manifestation; the dove signifies unity/peace and shows the Trinity; Gregory: the Spirit abides on the Mediator alone.
The dove is the Semitic symbol of the Spirit; John's perfect 'I have beheld' marks the sign as abiding proof, presupposing the Synoptic baptism.
Calvin reads the seeing sacramentally (sign and thing); the dove displays the Messiah's mildness; Christ receives the Spirit for his people.
Chrysostom: the descent manifests Christ, not supplies a lack, and fixes the Father's voice on him.
The descending Spirit was the God-given, infallible sign; John baptizes in water, the Messiah in the Holy Spirit.
Calvin resolves Matthew 3:14: the 'knowledge' denied is intimacy; the sign confirms for our sake, like the token to Moses.
Augustine: John is true because sent by the Truth (Christ); the inseparable Trinity works in his sending.
Cyril: the heavens opened at the dignity of the One descending; the dove befits the innocent Spirit.

debates.question: If John 'knew him not,' why did he say 'I have need to be baptized of thee'?

sides.position: the 'knowledge' denied is personal intimacy; the dove was a confirming sign for our sake
the sign was simply how John would infallibly recognise the Messiah

schools.summary: Perfect tenses mark an abiding eyewitness testimony; 'Son of God' is a Messianic title that also names the peculiar Son-Father relation.
Calvin: the giver of the Spirit must be the Christ who reconciles men to God, so John's sight certifies the divine Sonship.
Augustine sounds a contemplative note: the eternal Word alone abides and is worth loving.

outliers.note: His Catena excerpt is a pastoral meditation on loving the abiding Word rather than direct exegesis of the title.

schools.summary: Augustine: John the friend of the bridegroom points his disciples away to Christ; Bede and Alcuin read it mystically (Law standing, Jesus walking).
Chrysostom: John waits by the river so Christ's own works may draw men, kindling a spark into flame.
'Again' refers to v.29; the two disciples are Andrew (v.40) and the unnamed Beloved Disciple John, with an inner circle taught more.
The fixed gaze (emblepsas) pictures John's rapt contemplation; his last glimpse of Jesus, repeating the tribute of v.29; a model for ministers.
Calvin: John resigns his office to Christ; the church's small beginning magnifies its glory; the aim is to find forgiveness of sins in Christ.
Chrysostom: the humbler word 'Behold the Lamb' won them; John as the bridegroom's friend silently hands the bride to Christ.
The ingressive aorist marks the decisive following; John's willingness to lose disciples to Christ proves his sincerity.
Augustine and Alcuin read 'Come and see' mystically: come by believing and working, then see by understanding.
Matthew Henry: Christ notices the first motion of a soul; 'What seek ye?' is the beginner's self-examination; now is the accepted time.
Origen counts the testimonies, John falling silent after six and Jesus asking 'seventhly.'

outliers.note: Numerological reading: six witnesses of John, then Jesus' seventh question.

schools.summary: The vivid 'turned'; 'What seek ye?' as the first words of Jesus in John; the evangelist translates 'Rabbi' for Greek/late readers; they seek a place for converse.
Calvin: 'Rabbi' marks them as learners taking the first step; seek to lodge with Christ, not smell the gospel at a distance.

outliers.note: Renders 'where dwellest thou' as 'where keepest thou sabbath?' and conjectures a sabbath-eve.

schools.summary: Divided on the 'tenth hour': Roman 10 a.m. (Clarke, Robertson) vs Jewish 4 p.m. (Ellicott), with Barnes leaving it open; all keep the devotional memory of the hour.
Calvin reads Jewish time: evening near, two hours till sunset, the disciples too eager to mind lodging.

debates.question: Is the 'tenth hour' reckoned by Roman or Jewish time?

sides.position: Roman time, about 10 a.m.
Jewish time, about 4 p.m.

schools.summary: The unnamed second disciple is John himself, veiling his identity; Andrew is identified by relation to the famous Peter.
Calvin: faith spreads the light; Andrew with a spark enlightens his brother, and the eminent Peter is brought by the lesser Andrew.
Textual question of 'first' (proton) vs 'the first' (protos), implying John also found James; 'Messias' (Hebrew) only here and 4:25.
Augustine glosses Messias/Christus/Unctus, the Anointed; Bede presses to the name-change, Peter from petra.
Calvin grounds 'Messiah' in Daniel 9:25, appropriated to the Redeemer; marvels so few received the long-awaited One.
Grace is communicative; each convert seeks first his own household.
Origen harmonises this finding with the later seaside call.

debates.question: Does the text say Andrew found his brother 'first' (proton) or that he was 'the first' (protos) to do so?

sides.position: 'first' (proton) — implying John then found James
'the first' (protos) — Andrew the first to go after a brother

outliers.note: Harmonises the finding here with the later call by the Sea of Galilee.

schools.summary: Cephas (Aramaic) = Peter, a stone; the future 'shalt be called' is prophecy; the petra/petros distinction (though absent in Aramaic); Christ's omniscience foresees Peter's firmness.
Calvin: the name is prophetic of what Christ would make him; polemic against Rome — Peter is founded on Christ, not the church's foundation or head.

debates.question: Does the renaming make Peter the foundation/head of the church?

sides.position: No — Peter is himself built on Christ; the name magnifies grace, anticipating Matthew 16:18
It anchors Peter as the rock, by Christ's naming prerogative (Catena fathers)

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Philip, taught by none but Christ, obeys instantly; the voice of Christ kindles love; choice freely made stands firm; choice fruit out of Galilee.
Calvin: the efficacy of the gospel word — one word inflames Philip, yet preaching is fruitless unless secret grace quickens it.
Barnes distinguishes this conviction from the later apostolic call (Matthew 4:18); 'Follow me' is Christ's recurring summons.
Alcuin reads Bethsaida ('house of hunters') and Galilee ('transition') mystically; 'Follow me' calls to imitate Christ's humility.
Matthew Henry: preventing grace — 'Jesus findeth Philip'; Christ sought and found us first.
An eye-witness touch: Bethsaida of Galilee (distinct from Bethsaida Julias) explains the chain of calling among townsmen.
Calvin: the cursed town (Matthew 11:21) magnifies grace; JFB note the human means by which grace travels.
Philip's diligent search of Scripture (Deut 18, Gen 49); 'son of Joseph, of Nazareth' as common supposition; the evangelist's dramatic accuracy in giving words as spoken; Nathanael = Bartholomew.
Calvin: Philip's two errors are crowned because his sincere aim leads to the true Christ; better to stammer truly than eloquently introduce a false Christ.
Wesley: Philip thought him of Nazareth, not knowing of Bethlehem; Nathanael = Bartholomew.

themes: fulfilment of Moses and the prophets
Jesus of Nazareth / son of Joseph

schools.summary: Nazareth's poor repute and town-rivalry (cf. John 7:52); Clarke's view that Nathanael looked for Messiah from Bethlehem; 'Come and see' answers prejudice by examination.
Calvin: a twofold error — believing Philip's misstatement and contempt of the place, both Satan's stratagems; yet Nathanael lets inquiry correct him.
Wesley: guard against popular prejudice; integrity opened Nathanael to the force of evidence.
'Guile' (dolos) echoes Jacob's subtlety (Genesis 27:35 LXX); Nathanael an Israel without the supplanter's guile, at prayer under the fig tree (cf. Genesis 32:24, Isaiah 53:9).
Augustine: 'no guile' is not 'no sin'; guile is saying one thing and thinking another — Nathanael confesses sin rather than feigning righteousness.
Calvin: a definition of true Christianity, integrity vs hypocrisy; 'not all Israel who are of Israel' (Romans 9:6) forewarns of the nation's unbelief.
Chrysostom: Nathanael's honest hesitation, speaking neither for favour nor malice, shows him guileless and well-read in the prophets.
The fig tree as a place of private prayer; Christ sees not the body but the secret heart and worship; he judges character by private devotion (Psalm 139:2).
Calvin: to know hearts and see the hidden belongs to God alone; even when we think not of Christ, we are observed by him.

themes: secret prayer under the fig tree

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Nathanael meant 'Son of God' of a mere man (he narrows it to 'King of Israel'), unlike Peter's confession of very God, so is led to greater things.
Calvin: a real but limited confession; faith fixes on Christ's office and power as King, yet 'King of Israel' is still within narrow bounds.
Robertson and Barnes refuse to tone it down: both are Messianic titles (Psalm 2; cf. John 12:13), a noble acknowledgment that Christ is more than man.

debates.question: How deep is Nathanael's confession — full deity or Messiah-as-man?

sides.position: a true but limited/incipient faith, to be enlarged
a full and noble Messianic acknowledgment

schools.summary: 'Believest thou?' as question or (better) assertion (cf. John 20:29); the 'greater things' are the fuller proofs promised in v.51.
Calvin: not a reproof but approval of ready faith, to be confirmed by stronger arguments and a proof common to all.

Fulfils Jacob's ladder (Genesis 28:12); the doubled Amen marks Christ as 'the Amen'; heaven standing open = free intercourse (Isaiah 64:1; Acts 7:56); 'Son of man' = the race's representative, second Adam.
Calvin: not one time/place but a perpetual opened kingdom; angels minister to the whole Church for Christ's honour (Stephen, the mount, the Ascension).
Wesley leans to particular future events — Christ coming in glory with the angels, or angelic ministry at resurrection and ascension.
Cyril gathers from the doubled 'verily' the promise of eternal life by faith.

debates.question: Is the 'hereafter' a perpetual mediatorial state or a series of future events?

sides.position: a perpetual opened heaven / mediatorial intercourse in Christ
particular future events (glory, resurrection, ascension)

outliers.note: Cites the doubled 'verily' only for the catechesis on eternal life by faith.

themes: Christ the bond of heaven and earth
